skill.name: modern-javascript-patterns
skill.description: Guide for applying modern JavaScript patterns, including ES6+ syntax, async workflows, modules, iterators, generators, and functional programming techniques.
skill.summary: Modernize JavaScript code with ES6+ syntax, async patterns, modules, and functional techniques.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged dynamic imports, fetch calls, Markdown code fences, weak-crypto keywords, and reconnaissance-like terms. Review found these are documentation examples in SKILL.md, with no executable helper scripts, no prompt injection, and no evidence of malicious intent. The skill is safe to publish with low risk because users may copy network or dynamic import examples into their own projects.

low_findings.title: Documentation Examples Flagged as Dynamic Imports
low_findings.description: The dynamic import findings are JavaScript module-loading examples inside a fenced Markdown block. They load local relative modules and are not executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are inside a JavaScript documentation section and use relative paths such as ./math.js and ./feature.js. No executable wrapper or user-controlled import source was found.

low_findings.title: Documentation Examples Flagged as Network Access
low_findings.description: The fetch calls and resource URLs are educational examples or reference links. They do not transmit secrets and there is no code path that runs them automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network patterns are in instructional snippets and public resource links. I found no credential collection, external webhook, or hidden exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as External Commands
low_findings.description: The external command findings correspond to fenced JavaScript examples and code-block boundaries. They are not Ruby backtick execution or shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged regions are Markdown fenced code examples with JavaScript syntax. There is no shell invocation, child process API, or install script in the skill package.

low_findings.title: Keyword-Based Blocker Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography and reconnaissance blocker hits match ordinary words such as async/await, destructuring, id, password, find, and sync in documentation. No cryptographic function or system reconnaissance command was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows normal JavaScript tutorial text rather than MD5/SHA1 usage, host probing, credential access, or environment inspection. The scanner appears to have matched substrings and generic terms.

content.value_statement: Legacy JavaScript often becomes hard to read, test, and maintain. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code apply modern ES6+ syntax, async workflows, and functional patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains arrow functions, destructuring, spread, rest parameters, and template literals.
Guides migration from callbacks and Promise chains to async and await patterns.
Shows module imports, exports, dynamic imports, iterators, and generators.
Recommends functional array methods, composition, currying, and immutable updates.
Highlights performance patterns such as debouncing, throttling, and lazy evaluation.
Lists common JavaScript pitfalls and practical best practices.
limitations: It is a documentation-only skill and does not run automated refactors by itself.
Examples are generic and need adaptation to each project architecture.
It does not replace framework-specific guidance for React, Vue, Angular, or Node.js.
Network examples require normal security review before production use.

use_cases.title: Refactor Legacy Frontend Code
use_cases.description: Convert older functions, callbacks, and mutable updates into clearer ES6+ patterns.

use_cases.title: Teach Modern JavaScript Concepts
use_cases.description: Use concise examples to explain async workflows, modules, and functional transformations.

use_cases.title: Review JavaScript Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Check whether code uses readable patterns, handles async errors, and avoids common pitfalls.

prompt_templates.title: Modernize a Small Function
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the modern-javascript-patterns skill to refactor this JavaScript function with ES6+ syntax. Explain each change briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Async Error Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the modern-javascript-patterns skill to review this async JavaScript code. Improve error handling, parallelism, and readability.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Data Transformations
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the modern-javascript-patterns skill to convert this data processing code into clear functional JavaScript with immutable updates.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Legacy Modernization
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the modern-javascript-patterns skill to create a phased plan for modernizing this JavaScript module while preserving behavior and tests.

output_examples.input: A callback-based user loading function with nested success and error handlers.
output_examples.output: A clearer async function with a try and catch flow, direct return values, and notes about preserved behavior.

output_examples.input: A loop that mutates an array while filtering and transforming records.
output_examples.output: A functional pipeline using filtering, mapping, and immutable results, with a short explanation of readability benefits.

output_examples.input: A pull request that mixes legacy syntax, missing awaits, and repeated object updates.
output_examples.output: A review checklist that calls out async risks, syntax modernization opportunities, and safe refactor steps.

best_practices: Ask for behavior-preserving refactors and require tests or examples for changed code paths.
Provide the surrounding module context so recommendations match imports, exports, and runtime constraints.
Review copied fetch and dynamic import examples for project-specific security and bundling rules.
anti_patterns: Do not modernize syntax without checking runtime support and build tooling.
Do not replace every loop with array methods when readability or performance becomes worse.
Do not copy generic network examples into production without validation, authentication, and error handling.

faq.question: Does this skill execute JavaScript code?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guide with examples and recommendations for AI-assisted coding.

faq.question: Can it help migrate callbacks to async and await?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Promises, async and await, error handling, parallel execution, retries, and timeouts.

faq.question: Is it only for browser JavaScript?
faq.answer: No. Most patterns apply to browser and Node.js code, but framework details still need project context.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use it during code review?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill gives review criteria for readability, async safety, immutability, modules, and common pitfalls.

faq.question: Does it include TypeScript-specific guidance?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on modern JavaScript patterns and does not cover TypeScript typing strategies.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk low?
faq.answer: Static findings came from documentation examples, not executable behavior. Copyable network examples still deserve normal review.
